word_list.word: a fault confessed is half redressed
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈɑːlt kənfˈɛst ɪz hˈæf ɹiːdɹˈɛst

senses.definition: used to imply that by acknowledging and taking responsibility for one's fault, a person has already made progress toward resolving the issue

examples.x: The employee who made a mistake at work quickly admitted his fault, knowing that a fault confessed is half redressed, and worked hard to make amends for the error.

word_list.word: accusing the times is but excusing ourselves
word_list.pronunciation: ɐkjˈuːzɪŋ ðə tˈaɪmz ɪz bˌʌt ɛkskjˈuːsɪŋ aɪʊɹsˈɛlvz

senses.definition: used to say that blaming external circumstances or conditions for one's failures or shortcomings is just a way of making excuses for one's lack of effort, skill, or commitment

word_list.word: every bird (must|has to) hatch (her|its) own egg
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi bˈɜːd mˈʌst hɐz tʊ hˈætʃ hɜːɹ ɪts ˈoʊn ˈɛɡ

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of individuals taking responsibility for their own actions and outcomes

examples.x: As a mother, it's hard to see my children struggle, but I know that every bird must hatch her own egg.

word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹɪbˌɑːdiz bˈɪznəs ɪz bˈeɪsɪkli nˈoʊbɑːdiz bˈɪznəs

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of clearly assigning responsibilities, as when no one takes ownership of a task or issue, it tends to be neglected or ignored

word_list.word: success has many fathers, failure is an orphan
word_list.pronunciation: səksˈɛs hɐz mˈɛni fˈɑːðɚz fˈeɪlɪɹ ɪz ɐn ˈɔːɹfən

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of taking accountability in both success and failure, as people tend to claim credit for successes but disassociate themselves from failures

word_list.word: if you would be well served, (you might as well|) serve yourself
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf juː wʊd biː wˈɛl sˈɜːvd juː mˌaɪt æz wˈɛl sˈɜːv joːɹsˈɛlf

senses.definition: used to suggest that people should do things for themselves, since they cannot always rely on others to do things exactly the way they want

word_list.word: every tub must stand on its own bottom
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi tˈʌb mˈʌst stˈænd ˌɑːn ɪts ˈoʊn bˈɑːɾəm

senses.definition: used to emphasize that every person should be self-reliant and work toward achieving their own goals and meeting their own needs, rather than expecting others to do it for them

word_list.word: every man is (meant to be|) the architect of his own fortune
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi mˈæn ɪz mˈɛnt təbi ðɪ ˈɑːɹkɪtˌɛkt ʌv hɪz ˈoʊn fˈɔːɹtʃən

senses.definition: used as a reminder that individuals have the power to shape their own destiny through their choices and actions

examples.x: The entrepreneur knew that every man is the architect of his own fortune when he started his own business.

word_list.word: life is what you make it
word_list.pronunciation: lˈaɪf ɪz wˌʌt juː mˈeɪk ɪt

senses.definition: used to emphasize that a person's life is largely determined by their own choices, actions, and attitudes

examples.x: Despite facing many challenges, she believed that life is what you make it, and was determined to create a better future for herself.

senses.definition: used to advise individuals to prioritize their work or responsibilities before engaging in leisure activities or personal pursuits

examples.x: The student knew that she needed to finish her homework before going out with her friends, remembering the saying business before pleasure.

word_list.word: creditors have better memory than debtors
word_list.pronunciation: kɹˈɛdɪɾɚz hæv bˈɛɾɚ mˈɛmɚɹi ðɐn dˈɛɾɚz

senses.definition: used to imply that people who are owed money are more likely to remember the debt and pursue the repayment while those who owe the money may be inclined to forget or delay the payment

word_list.word: with great power comes great responsibility
word_list.pronunciation: wɪð ɡɹˈeɪt pˈaʊɚ kˈʌmz ɡɹˈeɪt ɹɪspˌɑːnsəbˈɪlɪɾi

senses.definition: used to imply that individuals who hold significant power or influence have a corresponding obligation to use it in a responsible and ethical manner for the greater good

examples.x: The politician recognized that with great power comes great responsibility, and used their influence to advocate for policies that would benefit the most vulnerable members of society.

word_list.word: the married man must turn his staff into a stake
word_list.pronunciation: ðə mˈæɹɪd mˈæn mˈʌst tˈɜːn hɪz stˈæf ˌɪntʊ ɐ stˈeɪk

senses.definition: used to emphasize the idea that once a person is married, they must prioritize and focus their efforts on providing for their family and fulfilling their obligations

examples.x: The old farmer often reminded his sons that once they said "I do", the married man must turn his staff into a stake and strive to provide a stable home for his spouse.

senses.definition: used to imply that  making a promise carries a moral obligation to fulfill it, similar to a financial debt that must be repaid

examples.x: The company CEO promised to increase employee salaries, but failed to do so, forgetting that promise is a debt and that breaking it can have negative consequences.

word_list.word: a dog is for life, not just for Christmas
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ dˈɑːɡ ɪz fɔːɹ lˈaɪf nˌɑːt dʒˈʌst fɔːɹ kɹˈɪsməs

senses.definition: used to emphasize that owning a dog is a long-term commitment that requires love, care, and responsibility throughout its life, rather than just as a holiday gift

word_list.word: let every fox take care of his own tail
word_list.pronunciation: lˈɛt ˈɛvɹi fˈɑːks tˈeɪk kˈɛɹ ʌv hɪz ˈoʊn tˈeɪl

senses.definition: used to imply that individuals should be responsible for their own actions and well-being, without relying on others to help or protect them

examples.x: After the accident, the company president told the employees to let every fox take care of his own tail and not to blame others for their mistakes.
